    Home › Types › Widebody airliners

    Widebody airliners

    Two aisles, two decks sometimes, and the backbone of long-haul travel.

    Aircraft here12
    Longest76.72 m
    Combined built9,682
    First flights1969–2020
    What it means

    The category, explained

    A widebody is any airliner with two aisles — a fuselage wide enough that passengers walk down two corridors instead of one, typically 5 to 6.5 metres across. The format was invented by the Boeing 747 and the Airbus A300 around 1970, and it made mass long-haul travel possible.

    Widebodies dominate the record boards for a simple reason: length and wingspan scale with the payload they carry across oceans. Every aircraft here carries a two-aisle cabin, and between them they fly the great majority of the world's intercontinental routes.

    The aircraft

    Every widebody airliner here

    Silhouettes are sized by real wingspan — bigger aircraft draw bigger.

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
